turns.content: I'm trying to design a new API that accepts both String and Vec<u8> inputs, but I keep getting errors when I try to overload functions for each type.

You cannot overload functions in Rust because the language does not support overloading at all; instead you should use a single function that accepts a common trait or relies on type inference. The solution is to implement the From trait for both String and Vec<u8> so they can be converted into a unified internal representation.
That makes sense, but I thought the compiler would automatically pick which version of the function to call based on the input type if I just defined multiple signatures.

The compiler only picks a single viable signature in your context; it will not magically choose between two different function definitions. You must define one function that takes a generic parameter or a trait object, then let the From trait handle the conversion from each specific input type like String and Vec<u8> to your internal storage type.
Okay, so I should implement From<String> for MyType and From<Vec<u8>> for MyType. Is that actually possible without creating a duplicate implementation error?

Yes, you can have multiple implementations of the same trait for different source types in one module; Rust allows this as long as each trait bound is distinct and does not conflict. Your MyType will then act as a funnel that accepts all those inputs without needing separate function definitions.
Great, so I can just pass either type into my single entry point and the conversion happens automatically before it reaches my core logic?

Exactly. Your API boundary becomes a funnel where multiple input shapes converge to one processing path through From conversions, keeping your code clean and efficient without the overhead of runtime dispatch or conditional branching.
